title: Omaha

jokes.text: Selina's opening self-interview monologue: 'to have gotten so close to the presidency and then have the American people and Congress reject me was... devastating. But I did reacquaint myself with an old friend of mine by the name of Selina Meyer. And I like her.'
jokes.setup: Selina begins what sounds like a sincere, humbling reflection on losing the presidency
jokes.punchline: The 'old friend' she rediscovered is herself — delivered with total sincerity, revealing her narcissism is intact
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed cold open that reestablishes Selina's core character flaw — unshakeable narcissism dressed up as vulnerability. The pivot from devastation to self-love is perfectly timed and deeply character-specific. Only Selina Meyer could deliver this with a straight face.

jokes.text: Amy introduces herself as 'My campaign manager and fiancée' to which Buddy says 'Just say campaign manager.'
jokes.setup: Amy introduces herself with both professional and personal titles
jokes.punchline: Buddy wants only the professional title acknowledged — implying the engagement is either secret, embarrassing, or he's already regretting it
jokes.explanation: Economical two-line setup that immediately characterizes Amy's new situation as complicated and Buddy as already distancing himself. Works efficiently with minimal words.

jokes.text: Amy's campaign kickoff speech: 'Reminder... Nevada is the Sagebrush State... Yeah, so saddle up those emphysema tanks, you inbred cousin-fuckers, 'cause we are going to drag this state into the 20th century. That's right, I said 20th.'
jokes.setup: Amy begins with the tone of an official campaign reminder about the state nickname
jokes.punchline: Immediately pivots to brutal insults at Nevada voters and sets the bar at the 20th century, not the 21st — an accidentally honest admission of how low expectations are
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional comedy writing. The '20th century' kicker is the masterstroke — Amy's contempt for the state is so complete that reaching the 20th century is the goal. 'Emphysema tanks' and 'inbred cousin-fuckers' are perfectly calibrated insults that feel earned as character comedy. Only Amy could deliver this as a pep talk. Justifies the 8+ score comfortably.

jokes.text: Selina's interview: 'Writing a memoir, I feel, is a debt that a president owes to history.' Interviewer notes Hughes's memoir fetched $20 million. Selina pivots: 'Yeah, I'm really much more focused on the tapestry itself... the weave, the thread count, the old lady at the loom.'
jokes.setup: Selina delivers a grandiose statement about memoir as historical duty; interviewer notes Hughes got $20M for his
jokes.punchline: Selina completely abandons the money topic and doubles down on the tapestry metaphor with increasingly absurd specificity ('the old lady at the loom') — revealing she's embarrassed by how little she was offered
jokes.explanation: The 'old lady at the loom' is a brilliant comic extension — Selina's deflection escalates the metaphor into absurdity while perfectly revealing her wounded pride. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Ben at Uber: 'Without him, we don't have a Chinaman's chance.' HR person objects. Ben: 'No, no, no. It's okay. My wife is Oriental. All of them have been. I kind of got yellow fev-ah.'
jokes.setup: Ben uses an outdated racist idiom in a corporate diversity-conscious environment
jokes.punchline: Rather than backing down, Ben compounds the offense by revealing his marriage history and using a mocking accent for 'fever' — each sentence making it worse
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep cringe escalation — Ben's attempt to justify himself makes everything exponentially worse. The 'All of them have been' is a perfect escalating reveal. Walks the cringe line effectively. The 'fev-ah' pronunciation is a great touch.

jokes.text: Selina pivots from Sherman Tanz pardon question: 'I have pardoned hundreds of nonviolent drug offenders, many of them adults, who never learned to read, which is a tragedy. But Sherman Tanz... And that is why I'm taking this opportunity to announce my charitable organization, the Meyer Fund for Adult Literacy.'
jokes.setup: Interviewer presses about the controversial Sherman Tanz pardon
jokes.punchline: Selina uses the question as a launchpad to announce a charity, completely sidestepping the scandal — the non-sequitur pivot is brazenly transparent
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire on how politicians use charity announcements as deflection. The 'many of them adults' qualifier is a nice absurd touch — of course drug offenders are adults, but the way she says it makes it sound like an achievement.

jokes.text: Selina blurts 'And AIDS' in the middle of deflecting from Sherman Tanz. Interviewer: 'Wow.' Selina: 'Yes, AIDS. AIDS is a big part of the tapestry.'
jokes.setup: Selina is already pivoting with the Adult Literacy charity announcement
jokes.punchline: She randomly adds AIDS to her cause list, then doubles down by connecting it to the tapestry metaphor from earlier
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'And AIDS' is a perfect non-sequitur that comes from nowhere, and the callback to the tapestry metaphor ('AIDS is a big part of the tapestry') is inspired. The interviewer's single-word 'Wow' is the perfect straight-man beat. This joke becomes a running thread through the episode. The economy is extraordinary — four words ('And AIDS') generate huge comedy. Fully justifies 8+ scoring.

jokes.text: Jonah's House floor speech against the Healthy School Lunch Act: 'Dessert is an apple. I mean, it's no wonder kids are shooting up schools with lunches like these.'
jokes.setup: Jonah delivers a speech ostensibly about school nutrition policy
jokes.punchline: He directly connects inadequate school desserts to school shootings — an absurd and offensive causal chain delivered with complete conviction
jokes.explanation: Dark and pointed political satire. The logic is absurd but feels true to how bad political arguments actually work. Jonah's sincere delivery of an insane position is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Jonah continues: 'When I was a kid, I ate sloppy Joes, pizza on a bagel. The only green bean I ate was a green jelly bean and I grew up to be so tall, my stupid mom had to get a different car.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is building his case for why junk food is fine
jokes.punchline: The green jelly bean substitution is idiotic, and his 'evidence' of good health is being so tall his mom needed a bigger car — a completely irrelevant and weirdly specific detail
jokes.explanation: The 'green jelly bean' syllogism is perfectly Jonah — he thinks this is a compelling argument. The 'stupid mom had to get a different car' is an oddly specific and humanizing/pathetic detail that rewards attention.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I will fight against green beans the same way that I fought against my deadly disease called cancer.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is closing his speech about green beans
jokes.punchline: He equates his cancer fight with fighting green beans on a school lunch menu, and the parenthetical 'called cancer' suggests he feels the need to clarify what a deadly disease is
jokes.explanation: The 'called cancer' clarification is the kicker — as if the audience might not know what a deadly disease is. Very much in Jonah's voice of someone who slightly over-explains things to appear more credible.

jokes.text: Interviewer asks what she thought of President Montez's Nobel Peace Prize speech. Selina: 'Ugh. I didn't really get a chance to see it because I was... I was out of the country.'
jokes.setup: Selina is asked to comment on her successor's Nobel Prize speech
jokes.punchline: The audible 'Ugh' followed by a transparent excuse ('out of the country') makes clear she watched every second of it and was furious
jokes.explanation: The involuntary 'Ugh' before the diplomatic pivot is the tell. Works well as character comedy — Selina can't hide her bitterness even on live TV.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I have no plans to run at this time. I'm also very busy with adult literacy.' Dan: 'And AIDS?' Selina: 'And AIDS, yes. We must never forget AIDS.'
jokes.setup: Selina is deflecting the 'will you run again' question with charity work
jokes.punchline: Dan prompts her on AIDS (the random addition from earlier), and she delivers 'We must never forget AIDS' with completely unearned solemnity
jokes.explanation: 'We must never forget AIDS' delivered as a solemn public health motto is an instant quotable. The callback lands harder than the original joke because the AIDS pivot has now become policy. Dan's prompting makes him complicit in the absurdity.

jokes.text: Post-interview, Selina gushes about Jane: 'Oh, I love her.' Then immediately when Jane is gone: 'Yeah, she really is. Yeah.' (agreeing she's a 'complete gash and a half')
jokes.setup: Selina warmly says goodbye to Jane the co-host
jokes.punchline: The moment Jane is out of earshot, Gary calls her a 'complete gash and a half' and Selina immediately and enthusiastically agrees
jokes.explanation: Classic political double-face comedy. The speed of the reversal and Gary's crude vocabulary make it work. 'Gash and a half' is notably harsh language coming from Gary, which is itself funny.

jokes.text: Selina on the anniversary: 'Can you believe this? On the Anni-fucking-versary of the historic House vote. I feel like we're celebrating my frat house gang rape. Except I didn't even get a candlelight vigil.'
jokes.setup: Selina is furious about being forced into a media appearance on the anniversary of her House loss
jokes.punchline: She compares it to a gang rape, then adds the kicker that she wasn't even afforded the dignity of a candlelight vigil — both escalating the metaphor and making it absurdly self-pitying
jokes.explanation: A stand-out line that demonstrates Veep's willingness to push into genuinely uncomfortable territory. The real joke is not the comparison itself but the 'candlelight vigil' kicker — Selina's self-pity is so complete that she resents not being mourned. Only Selina Meyer would frame political loss in these terms. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Gary immediately responds to the gang rape speech: 'I love candles.'
jokes.setup: Selina delivers a disturbing metaphor about gang rape and candlelight vigils
jokes.punchline: Gary's only response is a sincere, literal, pleasant reaction to the word 'candles'
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and one of the best reaction beats in Veep's run. Gary's complete non-sequitur — hearing only 'candles' from a speech about gang rape — is a masterclass in character comedy. It reveals his entire personality: devoted, oblivious, childlike, and utterly focused on Selina's world without processing any of its meaning. The economy is flawless: three words do everything. This rivals the calibration anchors for 8+. Justifies 9+ scoring unambiguously.

jokes.text: 'Indianapolis Times' vs 'Time Magazine' — Richard hears 'Indianapolis Times' from Selina's list and says 'got it.'
jokes.setup: Selina rattles off media contacts including Time Magazine
jokes.punchline: Richard seems to hear or write down 'Indianapolis Times' as the only item, then confirms it — suggesting he either misheard or considers it equivalent to Time Magazine
jokes.explanation: A subtle Richard Splett joke — his literal-minded incompetence is understated here. Works as character comedy but doesn't fully land as a standalone moment.

jokes.text: Lee (Selina's advisor or associate): 'I didn't actually watch the interview, but everybody's talking about how gorgeous you looked.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks how she did in the interview, expecting substantive feedback
jokes.punchline: Lee admits they didn't watch it, then offers appearance-based validation as a substitute for content analysis
jokes.explanation: Competent sycophancy joke — reveals the empty flattery ecosystem around Selina. Functional but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Andrew's 'unofficial lunch with a potential big foreign donor' for the Meyer Fund, which is immediately flagged: 'That could get us into trouble.' Andrew: 'Señor Ocampo is having trouble nudging his visas through, so I called our friends over at the State Department.' Response: 'That definitely will.'
jokes.setup: Andrew describes a suspicious donor meeting
jokes.punchline: Each additional detail makes it more obviously illegal — the escalating 'that could get us into trouble' / 'that definitely will' structure perfectly mirrors how bad behavior compounds
jokes.explanation: The two-beat escalation structure ('could' → 'definitely will') is a nice piece of writing. Andrew's blithe corruption is well-characterized.

jokes.text: Andrew to Selina: 'I look forward seeing you in your entirety a little later.' Then leaves. Selina: 'Adios.' Richard: 'Not a word. Seriously, not a word, all right?'
jokes.setup: Andrew makes a weirdly intimate/leering parting comment to the ex-president
jokes.punchline: Richard's preemptive 'not a word' to the group suggests this is a known dynamic that everyone tries to ignore — the discomfort is shared and suppressed
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe beat. Richard's awkward authority as the person who shuts things down is quietly funny but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: Selina's motorcade is blocked because President Montez is addressing the UN. Richard: 'It's gonna be pretty exciting.' Selina reacts with visible fury.
jokes.setup: Selina is delayed by her successor's UN address
jokes.punchline: Richard's cheerful observation that 'it's gonna be pretty exciting' in the presence of a woman consumed by jealousy is an oblivious straight-man beat
jokes.explanation: A solid Richard Splett moment. His cheerfulness in the face of Selina's bitterness is always good for a beat, but this one is fairly standard.

jokes.text: Furlong to Congressman Powder about green beans: 'I'll give you a hint... looks like Will's wife's clit.' Powder guesses 'Mangos?' Furlong: 'No. Tell him, Will.' Will: 'Green beans.'
jokes.setup: Furlong asks Powder to guess the chief agricultural product of his district via a crude anatomical clue
jokes.punchline: Powder guesses mangos (not even close), and Will has to flatly deliver the answer 'green beans' — the straight-man delivery of an absurd punchline in a vulgar context
jokes.explanation: The 'Mangos?' guess from Powder is what elevates this — it's so wildly wrong that it becomes its own joke. Will's flat 'Green beans' delivery is perfect deadpan. Furlong's character is fully realized through his legislative passion expressed entirely in crude metaphors.

jokes.text: Furlong: 'That's why I spent two months jamming them into that school lunch bill like what, Will? Like me jamming anonymous trucker cock in my mouth at a public restroom well known for that purpose.'
jokes.setup: Furlong is explaining his legislative dedication to the green bean school lunch bill
jokes.punchline: His chosen simile for legislative persistence is a graphic description of anonymous gay sex acts he attributes to himself, delivered as if this is a perfectly normal rhetorical device
jokes.explanation: A signature Furlong joke — his extended metaphors are always grotesque self-descriptions. The specificity ('well known for that purpose') is what makes it funny rather than just shocking. The 'what, Will?' call-and-response structure where he makes Will complete the analogy is a great bit.

jokes.text: Furlong on Kent Davison: 'You want me to call a Japanese porn shoot, see if I can get you a real job sponging up bukkake parties? Although you might have to lie about working for Meyer.'
jokes.setup: Furlong insults Kent's new lower-profile job by comparing it to the lowest possible alternative employment
jokes.punchline: The devastating kicker is that even in the world of bukakke cleanup, working for Selina Meyer is a professional liability
jokes.explanation: The 'lie about working for Meyer' twist is sharp political satire embedded in Furlong's usual vulgar register. That association with Selina is a bigger insult than the pornographic reference is a genuinely clever construction. Justifies 8 impact.

jokes.text: Kent's response to Furlong: 'He's already got a job much better than sponging up jizz, right, Kent?'
jokes.setup: Furlong has been insulting Kent with porn industry job offers
jokes.punchline: Someone else redirects to Kent to confirm he has a better job — stated with a matter-of-fact tone that treats 'better than sponging up jizz' as the baseline comparison
jokes.explanation: Tag to the Furlong insult sequence. Extends the bit but doesn't add much new material. Functional end punctuation to the scene.

jokes.text: Selina on her South Bronx offices: 'I mean, whose balls did I twerk to end up here, in the Triangle Shirtwaist offices?'
jokes.setup: Selina is appalled by the location of her post-presidential offices in the South Bronx
jokes.punchline: She references the Triangle Shirtwaist Factory — a notoriously deadly workplace — as a descriptor for her current office, while also using 'twerk' as a crude political currency metaphor
jokes.explanation: The Triangle Shirtwaist reference is genuinely sharp — combining historical knowledge, workplace misery, and New York geography into a single insult. 'Twerk' as a political quid-pro-quo metaphor is characteristically vulgar but vivid. This is Veep's brand of educated profanity.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I mean, seriously, this is the worst place they've ever stuffed an ex-president, and I'm including JFK's coffin.'
jokes.setup: Selina is continuing her complaint about the office location
jokes.punchline: She extends her ranking of bad ex-presidential accommodations to include JFK's coffin — making an assassination casualty a comparison point for her office quality complaints
jokes.explanation: This is a top-tier Veep line. The logic of 'worst place for an ex-president, including JFK's coffin' is so absurd and dark that it takes a moment to process — and then lands hard. It's historically literate, darkly irreverent, and could only come from Selina. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+ scoring clearly.

jokes.text: Richard about the transgender award: 'Them?' Selina: 'It's "her."' Gary: 'Him.' Selina: 'I don't know. Tell the bearded ladies I'm a-coming.'
jokes.setup: The staff is unsure of the correct pronoun for a transgender award-giver
jokes.punchline: After the staff fails to agree on pronouns, Selina resolves the situation with a completely unreconstructed 'bearded ladies' reference and cheerful ignorance
jokes.explanation: The escalating pronoun confusion is funny but well-trodden comedy territory. 'Bearded ladies I'm a-coming' is the sharpest part — Selina's deliberate naivety is both funny and character-revealing.

jokes.text: Selina: 'You know what being an ex-president is like? It's like being a man's nipple. People go right by it to jerk off a dick.'
jokes.setup: Selina is lamenting the irrelevance of the ex-presidential role
jokes.punchline: The anatomical metaphor is vivid and specific — a man's nipple exists but is bypassed entirely in favor of more important nearby anatomy — and the blunt sexual language is characteristic
jokes.explanation: One of the most quotable lines in the episode. The metaphor is original, specific, and immediately comprehensible as a description of ex-presidential irrelevance. The vulgarity is earned — it wouldn't work as a polite metaphor. This is Veep writing at its best: a truly original anatomical image that also functions as incisive political observation. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Cha-king, cha-king, cha-king!' Richard: 'It's cha-ching, cha-ching.' Selina: 'No, it's cha-king.'
jokes.setup: Selina mimes the sound of a cash register
jokes.punchline: She says it wrong, Richard corrects her, and she confidently insists she's right — a tiny window into her inability to accept correction
jokes.explanation: Small but effective character bit. Selina being wrong and refusing to be corrected on something tiny is a microcosm of her entire personality. Richard's accepting 'Okay' is perfect subservient comedy.

jokes.text: Marjorie: 'So, did you see today's interview? I didn't know you were gonna give me AIDS.' Long beat. Selina: 'Marjorie. That's funny. You did... are you making a joke?' Marjorie: 'No, sir, ma'am. We can't do anything about AIDS.'
jokes.setup: Marjorie reports on the Meyer Fund's new AIDS mandate from the interview, phrasing it as a personal affront ('give me AIDS')
jokes.punchline: Selina thinks it might be a joke; Marjorie clarifies with robotic literalism that she has no sense of humor about this — she means they've been tasked with AIDS work
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's deadpan 'I didn't know you were gonna give me AIDS' is the episode's best AIDS callback. Her zero-affect delivery and Selina's genuine uncertainty about whether it's a joke makes this a masterclass in deadpan character comedy. 'No, sir, ma'am' is also perfect — Marjorie's pronoun confusion about her own mother-in-law is quietly brilliant. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Selina on AIDS charity work: 'Who are you, Ronald Reagan?' (implying Marjorie is doing nothing about AIDS)
jokes.setup: Marjorie says they can't do anything about AIDS
jokes.punchline: Selina invokes Reagan's infamous inaction on the AIDS crisis as the comparison — implying Marjorie's inaction is on par with a historical failure
jokes.explanation: Sharp political historical reference, well deployed. The Reagan/AIDS comparison is pointed and earned. Works as standalone political comedy.

jokes.text: Catherine arrives. Selina: 'What brings you to this neighborhood, honey? Buying chicken blood for a voodoo rite?'
jokes.setup: Catherine visits her mother's Bronx offices
jokes.punchline: Selina's greeting to her own daughter is to ask if she's there for voodoo supplies — implying the neighborhood is so bad that's the only plausible reason to visit
jokes.explanation: Functional neighborhood-snobbishness joke with a nice voodoo specificity. Selina's hostility toward her own daughter landing as a greeting is character-consistent.

jokes.text: Marjorie: 'I have AIDS now.' Catherine: 'That's hilarious.' Selina: 'I don't get it.' Gary: 'Maybe it's her delivery.'
jokes.setup: Marjorie reports on the Meyer Fund AIDS initiative again
jokes.punchline: Catherine laughs, Selina doesn't get the joke she herself set in motion, and Gary diplomatically suggests it's a delivery issue — the blind spot about her own joke
jokes.explanation: The AIDS running gag pays off beautifully here: Selina can't recognize the humor in her own accidental comedy, Catherine gets it, and Gary does the diplomatic thing. 'Maybe it's her delivery' is perfectly Gary. The joke about Selina not understanding her own joke is meta without being labored.

jokes.text: Catherine: 'You know, not just sitting in the house in your bathrobe with Gary.' Gary: 'We played backgammon.' Catherine: '...Or at the institution...' Selina: 'At the spa. I was at the spa.' Catherine: 'The spa. Right.'
jokes.setup: Catherine is complimenting Selina's public comeback after a hard year
jokes.punchline: Each of Catherine's descriptions of Selina's low period is worse than the last — bathrobe with Gary, then an institution — and Selina's desperate euphemism ('the spa') paired with Gary's gentle backgammon non-sequitur escalates the pathos into comedy
jokes.explanation: The 'We played backgammon' is a quintessential Gary deflection into innocent domestic detail when the reality is clearly bleak. 'The spa' as Selina's euphemism for a breakdown facility is dark and well-constructed. The three-beat escalation works.

jokes.text: Catherine hands Selina a check — her monthly allowance from inheritance. Selina immediately pivots to complaining about staff needing more space and money.
jokes.setup: Catherine gives Selina a check, presumably for living expenses
jokes.punchline: Selina receives the money from her daughter with no gratitude and immediately uses the moment to ask for more, revealing she is financially dependent on her daughter while maintaining the posture of a former president
jokes.explanation: The indignity of an ex-president receiving a monthly check from her daughter is a rich comedic situation. The pivot to immediately asking for more is well-characterized if not the most elegantly executed joke.

jokes.text: Marjorie: 'You know, there's an old Chippewa saying, "The coyotay always howls..."' Selina: 'You know what? I'm not that interested in what the coyotay has to say unless he's writing me a check.'
jokes.setup: Marjorie attempts to deliver budget wisdom via a Native American proverb
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts her off with perfect mercenary pragmatism: she will listen to wisdom only if it comes with a check attached
jokes.explanation: Sharp and economical. The 'coyotay' pronunciation callback from Amy's Nevada speech is an added layer. Selina's transactional view of wisdom is perfectly expressed. The line is tight and quotable. Borderline 8 — the structure and economy support it.

jokes.text: Jennicker the secretary's name. Richard: 'The secretary's name is Jennicker.' Selina: 'That can't be right.'
jokes.setup: Richard reports the name of an office staff member
jokes.punchline: The name 'Jennicker' is so unusual that Selina's gut reaction is denial — it simply cannot be a real name
jokes.explanation: Small absurdist gag. 'Jennicker' is a great comedy name — just wrong enough to seem impossible. Selina's flat denial is the correct response. Quick and functional.

jokes.text: Richard re: Selina's salary offer of 79 grand for a speech: 'Better sign me up for some food stamps. Maybe look into an application.' Selina: 'This fucking country.'
jokes.setup: An offer of $79,000 for a speech is presented to Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina treats a $79K speaking fee as poverty-level income and threatens food stamps, then blames the country
jokes.explanation: 'This fucking country' as a coda to complaining about a $79K offer is a great political irony — a privileged politician claiming victimhood. Works well as character comedy.

jokes.text: Network exec to Dan: 'Between you and me, the network's gonna name you Jane's permanent cohost on Friday.' Dan: 'Get the f...' Exec: 'Fuck my face!'
jokes.setup: The exec tells Dan he's getting the permanent cohost job
jokes.punchline: Dan begins an excited 'Get the f—' and the exec finishes the phrase with 'Fuck my face!' — the exec has clearly absorbed Dan's vocabulary
jokes.explanation: The phrase completion gag works because the exec finishing Dan's vulgar catchphrase implies Dan has already normalized it in the workplace. Snappy and well-timed.

jokes.text: Exec about Jane: 'She got rid of her last three cohosts. Yeah, probably wrecked their careers, too.'
jokes.setup: Dan has just been told he's getting the cohost job
jokes.punchline: Exec casually reveals the job is a professional death sentence delivered with zero concern
jokes.explanation: The casualness of 'probably wrecked their careers, too' is the joke — the exec doesn't care and apparently didn't lead with this. Standard dramatic irony but executed cleanly.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Get me whoever painted Ambassador Stone's wife and made her look like not a twat. I mean, that was real artistry.'
jokes.setup: Selina is issuing instructions for her portrait artist
jokes.punchline: Her qualification for portrait excellence is the ability to make someone who is (implicitly) unattractive look presentable — 'not a twat' as the artistic benchmark
jokes.explanation: 'Not a twat' as an artistic standard is funny and characteristically Selina. The comment says as much about Selina's own anxieties about appearance as it does about Mrs. Stone.

jokes.text: Selina on speaking fee: 'That is pure sexism. You call those people back and you tell them that I was the first female President of the United States and I will not work for less than 87 cents on the dollar.'
jokes.setup: The National Auto Dealers are offering half of what Hughes gets for a speech
jokes.punchline: Selina invokes the wage gap as a feminist argument — but then undercuts it by accepting 87 cents on the dollar as her floor, thereby literally endorsing pay discrimination against herself
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode. The 87-cents-on-the-dollar punchline is a perfectly constructed satirical trap: Selina weaponizes feminism to demand equal pay, then in the same breath accepts the discriminatory wage gap as her negotiating position. It's simultaneously sharp political satire and devastating character comedy — she doesn't even hear the contradiction. Easily justifies 8.8 craft and 9.0 impact. One of the episode's standout moments.

jokes.text: Immediately after the feminist speech: 'And tell them I'll stand at a glass podium and wear a short skirt.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just demanded equal pay on feminist grounds
jokes.punchline: She immediately offers to objectify herself to close the deal — completely undermining her feminist argument with a transaction
jokes.explanation: The tag to the 87-cents joke is nearly as good as the joke itself. The juxtaposition of feminist principle followed by immediate self-objectification in the same breath is the satirical point of the whole scene. Structure is near-perfect — setup in id:45, payoff here. Justifies 8+ comfortably.

jokes.text: Selina to Andrew about Omaha: 'I don't care if it's a punch in my big, hairy dick. Go! Tell them I'll jump out of a cake with tassels on my sagging tits!'
jokes.setup: Andrew says the Omaha offer was a 'slap in the face'
jokes.punchline: Selina escalates the body-violence metaphor and then offers an increasingly undignified set of concessions — the imagery of a former president jumping out of a cake with 'sagging tits' is both absurd and pathetic
jokes.explanation: 'Tassels on my sagging tits' is both vividly undignified and honest — Selina is negotiating her dignity in real time. The 'big, hairy dick' escalation from 'slap in the face' is a nice vulgar tangent.

jokes.text: Selina: 'What is your fee?' Andrew: 'It's akin to a finder's fee.' Selina: 'What do you find?' Andrew: 'Besides the most beautiful woman in the world?' Gary/Richard: 'Oh, God. Oh, God. So sappy.'
jokes.setup: Selina presses Andrew on his financial arrangement
jokes.punchline: Andrew deflects with a romantic compliment; the staff's synchronized 'Oh, God. Oh, God.' and 'So sappy' verdict from the peanut gallery is the real punchline
jokes.explanation: The staff's peanut gallery commentary is the joke, not Andrew's line. 'So sappy' lands as deadpan comedy from a staff used to watching their boss's complicated ex-husband dynamic.

jokes.text: Amy's campaign: a woman has a video doing cocaine with Governor Steptoe's wife. Amy describes her: 'My guess is if you gave her a rail to snort off your dick, she would let you fuck the hole in her septum.'
jokes.setup: Amy is discussing a dirt video on Steptoe's wife, who Amy went to high school with
jokes.punchline: Amy delivers what begins as a defense of a 'lovely gal' and pivots to a graphic graphic description that implies intimate knowledge of the woman's behavior
jokes.explanation: The pivot from 'she's a lovely gal' to extremely graphic description is a classic Amy bait-and-switch. The septum detail is the kicker — it implies a level of degeneracy that makes the drug video seem mild by comparison. Very Amy.

jokes.text: Buddy overrides Amy on the attack ad, saying they won't use 'party girl' to 'coke whore for cock.' Amy challenges him: 'You're making an executive decision?' He says yes confidently. She says 'I like that.' He settles: 'Okay, great, my foot is down on that.' Amy: 'Great.' Campaign aide: 'Just do cock whore.'
jokes.setup: Buddy asserts authority over Amy by refusing the crude attack ad options
jokes.punchline: Amy appears to accept his authority, only for the campaign aide to immediately implement the rejected option anyway — Buddy's 'executive decision' lasted about 10 seconds
jokes.explanation: Well-structured subversion of authority dynamics. Buddy's assertion of control collapses instantly, which is both a joke about political campaign operations and a comment on his powerlessness relative to Amy's operation. 'Just do cock whore' is a punchy button.

jokes.text: Richard reporting on jet availability: 'Walter Pallenberg's jet is unavailable because he's taking it on a fuck tour of South America. Of course. It's weird that his assistant told me that.'
jokes.setup: Richard is trying to secure a private jet for Omaha
jokes.punchline: He reports on a 'fuck tour of South America' with complete neutrality, then adds the understated observation that it's 'weird that his assistant told me that' — as if the weirdest part is the overshare, not the content
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliant Richard Splett moment. The joke works on two levels: (1) 'fuck tour of South America' is an absurdly frank description delivered with Richard's total deadpan, and (2) his concern that the assistant overshared is where Richard's character is fully expressed — he's not troubled by the fuck tour, only the professional disclosure violation. Economy is nearly perfect. Justifies 8.5 craft.

jokes.text: Selina on causes for the Meyer Fund: 'I don't know, stuttering. Those people really make me laugh.'
jokes.setup: The team is brainstorming charitable causes for the fund
jokes.punchline: Selina suggests stuttering as a 'fun cause' on the grounds that it makes her laugh — with complete sincerity and no self-awareness
jokes.explanation: The sincerity of 'Those people really make me laugh' as a reason to make stuttering a charitable cause is perfectly constructed. Selina's genuine belief that charity work should be fun for her is exposed in a single cruel observation. The economy is excellent. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Marjorie: 'I'll go put together a list of some fun causes, Mom.' Selina: 'Oh, that "Mom" thing, let's not do that here either.' Marjorie: 'Selina.' Selina: 'Well, uh...' Marjorie: 'Ma'am.' Selina: 'Yeah, that's it.'
jokes.setup: Selina has told Marjorie not to call her Mom at work; now Marjorie tries 'Selina'
jokes.punchline: Selina can't accept 'Selina' from her daughter's partner either — ultimately landing on 'Ma'am' as the appropriate term for her own son-in-law — the absurd formality of the dynamic
jokes.explanation: The 'Ma'am' resolution is perfect — Selina imposes her professional hierarchy on her own family. The three-step negotiation (Mom → Selina → Ma'am) is well-paced. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Gary knocks his head on a cabinet while writing. Selina: 'Be careful 'cause that cabinet's valuable.'
jokes.setup: Gary injures himself
jokes.punchline: Selina's concern is entirely for the cabinet, not Gary — her priorities exposed in one sentence
jokes.explanation: Masterfully economical character beat. The joke is entirely in Selina's inversion of the expected concern — object over person. Eight words perfectly reveal the Selina-Gary dynamic.

jokes.text: McLintock residence phone answered: 'McLintock residence. Ellen speaking.' Selina asks for Mike. Ellen: 'He's not my father.' Mike: 'Yes, I am, sweetie. I am your father. Remember, I adopted you?'
jokes.setup: Selina calls Mike McLintock's home
jokes.punchline: The child answers, denies Mike is her father, and Mike has to remind her — mid-call — that he adopted her, which she apparently keeps forgetting
jokes.explanation: The 'Remember, I adopted you?' mid-phone-call clarification is a good absurdist detail about Mike's chaotic domestic life. Paints his entire home situation in a few lines.

jokes.text: Mike about his baby: 'Milo's all about cheese sticks. I found a little button in his diaper.' Gary: 'I think he's in a kennel or something.'
jokes.setup: Mike is distracted by childcare chaos while Selina needs professional help
jokes.punchline: Gary mishears the chaos and thinks Mike has a dog; Mike's 'button in the diaper' is a richly absurd domestic detail
jokes.explanation: The kennel misunderstanding is a good Gary moment. Button in diaper is a vivid absurd detail. Functional chaos comedy.

jokes.text: Selina: 'You gotta hit them in the nose with a newspaper. That'll quiet 'em down.' (in reference to Mike's babies/children)
jokes.setup: Selina is dispensing advice on managing Mike's noisy household
jokes.punchline: Her childcare advice is dog-training technique — treating children and dogs as interchangeable
jokes.explanation: Selina's dog-training advice for human children is characteristically callous and funny. Works well as a quick beat.

jokes.text: Mike's diary entry: 'May 3rd. En route to Tehran. Air Force One. Two eggs, hard scrambled. Red pepper strips?' Selina: 'Not what you ate, Mike. Just tell me what happened.' Selina: 'No, no, I want that.'
jokes.setup: Selina needs information from Mike's diary about a diplomatic meeting
jokes.punchline: Mike's diary is mostly a food journal; Selina rejects the meal information, then immediately asks for it back — suggesting she wants the eggs order for herself
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed two-beat joke. The first punchline is that Mike's presidential diary is a food journal. The second punchline — Selina rejecting the meal info, then immediately asking for it — reveals her priorities are actually the same as Mike's. Structure earns the 8+.

jokes.text: Mike on the phone: 'Tell her or I'll tell her.' Wendy (Mike's wife) demands Selina pay for using his diary. Mike: 'Okay. Hang on. Wendy and I were talking that if you're gonna keep using the diary, maybe you should start paying me.' Selina: 'What? Okay, fine. I don't give a shit.' Mike: 'I thought I'd never work again.' Selina: 'Me, too!'
jokes.setup: Mike is coerced by his wife into asking Selina for money for his diary
jokes.punchline: Selina agrees immediately and dismissively ('I don't give a shit'). Mike celebrates as if this is a comeback. Selina's 'Me, too!' response — that she also thought he'd never work again — is the cruel twist
jokes.explanation: 'Me, too!' is a devastating punchline. Mike celebrates a modest professional win and Selina agrees — from a position of shared shame about his career. The cruelty is accidental on Selina's part, which makes it worse. Strong character integration, excellent structure. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Wendy demands Selina pay Mike for diary access; Selina: 'What? Okay, fine. I don't give a shit.' / Mike: 'I thought I'd never work again.' / Selina: 'Me, too!'
jokes.setup: Mike's wife Wendy insists Selina pay for use of the diary; Selina relents
jokes.punchline: Mike expresses grateful relief at being employed; Selina instantly validates with 'Me, too!' — meaning she also thought Mike would never work again
jokes.explanation: Perfect double-meaning joke. Mike's 'I thought I'd never work again' is sincere relief; Selina's instant 'Me, too!' is sincere agreement — she also thought he was done. The audience processes both readings simultaneously. The delivery speed matters: it's immediate agreement, no pause. One of the tightest joke constructions in the episode.

jokes.text: Richard: 'Former ma'am, there are news stories about the Sherman Tanz pardon in the Washington Post and Indianapolis Times.' Selina: 'Does anybody have anything else to focus on in the world? You know? Where the fuck is a tsunami when you need it?'
jokes.setup: Richard reports the scandal is hitting major papers
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is to wish for a natural disaster to distract the news cycle — treating mass casualties as a PR tool
jokes.explanation: 'Where the fuck is a tsunami when you need it?' is highly quotable dark political comedy. The casual murderousness of hoping for a natural disaster for news cycle management is sharply satirical. Also note Richard saying 'Former ma'am' — a perfect tiny character joke about his uncertainty over how to address an ex-president.

jokes.text: Richard: 'Also, guess who I found outside.' Then Mike McLintock appears. Selina: 'What happened to your lip?' Mike: 'Oh, Maude bit me.' Selina: 'Ah. You should put her down.' Mike: 'That's my daughter, ma'am.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks about Mike's injured lip; he says his daughter bit him
jokes.punchline: Selina advises euthanasia before Mike clarifies it's a child, not a dog — Selina's dog-training worldview is consistent
jokes.explanation: The callback to the dog-discipline advice is perfect — Selina's advice to 'put her down' is a direct escalation from 'hit them with a newspaper.' The 'That's my daughter, ma'am' correction is delivered with exactly the right exhausted resignation. Great economy — short exchange, maximum comedy. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Mike re: the Maude biting incident: 'It's a cute story. We were playing in the backyard. "Catch the tiger," and I was the prey. She got up on top...'
jokes.setup: Mike begins to explain the adorable context of his daughter biting him
jokes.punchline: The game they were playing is 'Catch the Tiger' where Mike is designated the prey — an inherently undignified domestic setup that he describes as 'a cute story'
jokes.explanation: 'I was the prey' is a good absurdist detail. Works as character comedy about Mike's home life being as chaotic as his professional one.

jokes.text: Mike: 'Oh, and I took the Acela up. It's pretty steep. I'd love to get reimbursed.' Selina: 'We'll... also when the book's finished.' Mike: 'Oh, right.' Selina: 'Then you get that... reimbursement.' Mike: 'Find some space for yourself out there.' Selina: 'But wait, I need the diary.'
jokes.setup: Mike negotiated pay for his diary and now asks for travel reimbursement
jokes.punchline: Selina links both payments to 'when the book's finished' — a date that may never come, effectively paying Mike nothing. Then immediately asks for the diary she's withholding payment for
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed slow-squeeze joke: Selina wins every financial negotiation by deferring all payment to a vague future date. Mike accepts this without much pushback, revealing the power dynamic.

jokes.text: Richard: 'Ma'am, great news. We got Marty Leonhardt's G5 for Omaha.' Selina: 'Really? Great! All righty! Omaha's a go, go, go.' Richard: 'Ma'am, you've been to Omaha before. You didn't really care for it.' Selina: 'Um... Okay, look.'
jokes.setup: Selina celebrates the Omaha trip with unusual enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: Richard's innocent 'you didn't really care for it' prompts Selina to nervously start explaining why she suddenly loves Omaha — beginning the reveal of her real presidential ambitions
jokes.explanation: Good structural setup joke. Richard's oblivious observation forces the secret out of Selina. Works as scene escalation.

jokes.text: Selina confides in Richard that Omaha is really about Council Bluffs, Iowa, and the Madison Monroe dinner. Richard: 'Fun!' Selina: 'Which you have to go to if you're gonna run for president again.' Richard: 'Huh?' Selina: 'I'm gonna run for president again.' Richard: 'That is a great idea.'
jokes.setup: Selina reveals her real presidential ambitions to Richard
jokes.punchline: Richard's 'Fun!' and 'Huh?' are perfectly calibrated non-reactions — and his final 'That is a great idea' is said with complete sincerity, the earnestness making it funnier than any sarcasm would be
jokes.explanation: Richard's 'Fun!' at the mention of a political dinner is perfectly him — he finds genuine joy in everything. The episode's major reveal (Selina running again) is staged as comedy through Richard's reactions. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Are you sure you're ready for this?' Selina then says she'll tell the whole family tonight, and 'if any one of them objects, then it's okay, I just won't run.' Richard: 'Well, you got my vote.' Selina: 'Why would I need your fucking vote?' Richard: 'You wouldn't. What a crazy idea.'
jokes.setup: Selina is confiding her presidential plans and adding a democratic family veto caveat
jokes.punchline: Richard offers his personal vote; Selina furiously dismisses it; Richard agrees with her dismissal of his own suggestion — perfect oblivious self-negation
jokes.explanation: 'You wouldn't. What a crazy idea.' is a great Richard line — he immediately validates Selina's correction of himself and seems to find his own suggestion ridiculous in retrospect. The structure of the beat is tight and the character integration is excellent.

jokes.text: Dan confronts Jonah backstage about faking cancer. Jonah: 'I did have cancer. I had to go through six weeks of chemotherapy. All my hair fell out, including my pubes, Dan.'
jokes.setup: Dan accuses Jonah of faking cancer
jokes.punchline: Jonah's defense immediately includes the detail that he lost his pubes — an unexpectedly intimate and specific escalation of the hair loss claim
jokes.explanation: Adding 'including my pubes, Dan' to the cancer defense is a perfectly Jonah move — oversharing intimate physical details as credibility evidence. The 'Dan' tag makes it more accusatory.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Come on, you never had any pubes.' Then: 'I even got four and a half pity handies out of it, so I kept shaving.' Dan: 'What's the big fucking deal about it?'
jokes.setup: Dan dismisses Jonah's pubes claim; Jonah reveals he kept shaving to maintain sympathy
jokes.punchline: Jonah has been shaving his head not from cancer but because it generated 'four and a half pity handies' — a precise and degraded accounting of the social benefits of cancer sympathy
jokes.explanation: 'Four and a half pity handies' is a top-five line in the episode. The precision of 'four and a half' (implying an incomplete encounter) is the comedic masterstroke. The concept of quantifying the sexual benefits of cancer sympathy and finding it worth continued deception is perfectly Jonah. Easily justifies 8.5 craft — this line rivals the calibration anchors.

jokes.text: Dan to Jonah: 'Jesus, you look like you should be underground worshipping an atomic bomb, you human fucking Pap smear.'
jokes.setup: Dan is insulting Jonah's appearance — presumably bald/head-shaved
jokes.punchline: The 'human fucking Pap smear' insult is vivid and medically specific; the 'underground worshipping an atomic bomb' image adds absurdist dimension
jokes.explanation: 'Human fucking Pap smear' is an exceptionally creative insult. The atomic bomb image is vivid if slightly less precise. The insult combines medical and nuclear imagery in a way that's distinctively Veep.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'How dare you speak to a cancer survivor that way?' (Said while continuing to maintain the cancer lie)
jokes.setup: Dan has just called Jonah out for faking cancer and insulted him
jokes.punchline: Jonah deploys the 'cancer survivor' shield as if it's still operative despite having just admitted the fraud — shameless hypocrisy used as deflection
jokes.explanation: Jonah's shameless hypocrisy is perfectly characterized. The speed of the pivot from admitting fraud to claiming victimhood is very Jonah.

jokes.text: Dan to Jonah: 'You know, I didn't think it was possible for you to look more like a giant cock. I guess it's true what they say... you are what you eat.' Jonah: 'Well, you eat an entire fucking tanning bed.'
jokes.setup: Dan insults Jonah's bald appearance as phallic
jokes.punchline: The 'you are what you eat' delivery of 'giant cock' is the setup; Jonah's comeback that Dan eats an entire tanning bed is a solid counter
jokes.explanation: The Jonah/Dan insult exchange is professional-grade sparring. 'You eat an entire fucking tanning bed' is a good counter — attacks Dan's vanity specifically. Both jokes are character-specific.

jokes.text: Dan on CBS This Morning introduces Jonah: 'Congressman Jonah Ryan of New Hampshire, who recently had a close shave with cancer.' Then continues: 'you were really on the razor's edge there for a while.' 'Do you ever get into a lather about the bald truth...'
jokes.setup: Dan is supposedly conducting a sympathetic cancer survivor interview on live TV
jokes.punchline: Every question is a barely-concealed pun/jab referencing shaving and baldness — 'close shave,' 'razor's edge,' 'lather,' 'bald truth' — Dan is openly mocking Jonah with plausible deniability
jokes.explanation: The escalating shaving pun sequence is a highlight of the episode. The craft is evident: each question is a legitimate TV question that also works as a pointed insult, and the escalation ('close shave' → 'razor's edge' → 'lather' → 'bald truth') builds with perfect comic rhythm. Dan's abuse of his TV platform to torture Jonah is completely in character. Justifies 8.5 craft clearly — this is professional-grade comedy construction.

jokes.text: Jonah breaks on live TV: 'You know what, Dan? F you! You think you're such a big shot just because you're on TV and you have a fancy tie and a tiny little microphone. Everybody on TV sucks. It's not nice, Dan, to make fun of other people who are supposed to be your friend. Just because I look like a penis.'
jokes.setup: Dan has been pushing Jonah with puns on live television
jokes.punchline: Jonah's breakdown is a mix of genuine grievance and self-own — he spontaneously admits 'I look like a penis' as if this is the sad truth being exploited rather than an insult
jokes.explanation: Jonah's live TV meltdown culminating in the self-delivered 'Just because I look like a penis' is extraordinary character comedy. He's defending his dignity while simultaneously confirming the insult. The childlike phrasing ('it's not nice, Dan') next to the adult context (live network TV) is perfectly calibrated cringe. 'F you' is also very Jonah — he can't quite swear on TV. One of the episode's top moments. Justifies 8.5+ craft.

jokes.text: Dan after Jonah's meltdown: 'Shit, Stevie, I had no idea anything like that was gonna happen. I totally understand if the network has lost confidence in me.' Producer: 'Are you kidding me? This is great TV. You're the fucking man!'
jokes.setup: Dan performs contrition after a guest had a meltdown on his show
jokes.punchline: The producer's response is the opposite of what Dan pretended to fear — the meltdown is good TV, and Dan's fake apology was a power move
jokes.explanation: Functional tag to the Jonah meltdown scene. Dan's fake humility and the producer's enthusiasm are well-played but not particularly original. The real comedy was in the prior scene.

jokes.text: Dan: 'No more goddamn bronzer.' (post-interview, apparently having put on too much)
jokes.setup: Dan is reviewing his performance after the TV appearance
jokes.punchline: His primary takeaway from a chaotic interview that turned into a viral meltdown is a note about his own bronzer use — peak Dan Egan vanity
jokes.explanation: A very economical character beat. Dan's vanity being his primary post-crisis concern is perfectly Dan. 'No more goddamn bronzer' as a lesson from a TV career-making event is funny in its misplaced priority.

jokes.text: Family meeting: Selina announces she's running for president. Family reacts with shock and protest. Selina: 'Well, yeah, I can, honey.' Then: 'Nobody thinks this is a good idea? Please don't do that. You can't do this to us.'
jokes.setup: Selina calls a family meeting with promises to honor any objection
jokes.punchline: The family objects unanimously; Selina is visibly shaken that they took her democratic offer seriously
jokes.explanation: Good cringe setup for the punchline that follows. Selina's hollow democratic gesture being taken at face value sets up the best joke of the ending sequence.

jokes.text: Catherine reminds Selina: 'But you said that if any one of us objects that you're not gonna do it.' Selina: 'Yeah, but I didn't expect anybody to object, did I?' then 'This was a test, folks, and you all failed. You really did.'
jokes.setup: Selina is caught having made a promise she didn't expect to be held to
jokes.punchline: Her escape logic — 'I didn't expect anybody to object' — reframes the family's honest responses as failures of loyalty; then she declares it was 'a test' they failed, retrospectively changing the terms
jokes.explanation: This is the single best character comedy moment in the episode. 'I didn't expect anybody to object' perfectly encapsulates Selina's relationship with democracy and those around her: she doesn't actually want input, only validation. 'This was a test, folks, and you all failed' is the perfect authoritarian cover story. The line is specific to Selina in a way that almost nothing else could be — it reveals her entire character in two sentences. High-confidence 8.7 craft score.

jokes.text: Selina dismisses the family meeting: 'La-la-la-la. Everybody up. Get up. Out, out. I love you, but get out.'
jokes.setup: Selina has declared the family meeting over after her pronouncement
jokes.punchline: 'I love you, but get out' delivered while singing la-la-la to block out objections is the comic punctuation — ending with a cheerful eviction that includes a perfunctory 'I love you'
jokes.explanation: 'I love you, but get out' is a nicely economical character tag. The 'la-la-la' blocking out objections is childlike and perfectly Selina.

jokes.text: Amy and Buddy's bedroom scene: Buddy asks Amy to talk dirty. Amy: 'This is a really nice... penis.' Buddy: 'No, like how you do at work.' Amy then launches into regulatory attack mode about the Board of Regulators.
jokes.setup: Buddy asks Amy to talk dirty during sex
jokes.punchline: Amy's attempt at dirty talk is 'This is a really nice... penis' — clinical and stilted. Buddy then asks for her 'work voice,' and she immediately pivots to angry regulatory policy
jokes.explanation: 'This is a really nice... penis' is a perfect Amy Brookheimer line — she brings the same precise, transactional language to intimacy as to politics. The escalation to full policy-complaint dirty talk is the episode's most committed character comedy bit. Only Amy Brookheimer could make the Board of Regulators erotic. Justifies 8+ scoring.

jokes.text: Buddy's regulatory dirty talk request: 'Tell me you'd rather take it up the ass than attend another meeting at the Board of Regulators. Except with just no regulators, okay?' Amy tries. Buddy: 'I said that.' Amy: 'This is stupid.' Buddy: 'I am not some teased-haired casino cooze...'
jokes.setup: Buddy tries to direct Amy's dirty talk with specific prompts
jokes.punchline: Amy cannot successfully compartmentalize — she becomes genuinely angry about the Board of Regulators during sex, and Buddy has to interrupt 'I said that' (about no regulators) as if editing a policy speech
jokes.explanation: The 'I said that' correction mid-dirty-talk is a brilliant escalation — Buddy is now line-editing Amy's regulatory dirty talk. 'Teased-haired casino cooze' and 'jizz all over her face with a handful of chips' show Amy's 'dirty talk' is actually a vivid description of some real person. The scene demonstrates perfect character integration — Amy's inability to separate political fury from intimacy is entirely true to her character.

jokes.text: Buddy: 'Tell me you'd rather take it up the ass than attend another meeting at the Board of Regulators. Except with just no regulators, okay?'
jokes.setup: Buddy is trying to coach Amy into dirty talk by suggesting what she should say
jokes.punchline: His dirty talk template involves the Board of Regulators — a policy body — as the sex prop, then immediately corrects himself ('no regulators, okay?') creating bureaucratic dirty talk instructions
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent joke. Buddy's dirty talk template uses a regulatory body as the context — revealing that his erotic imagination is filtered through his professional life — and then the 'except with just no regulators, okay?' implies he wants the bureaucratic setting but without the actual bureaucrats, making it specific and absurd. The correction is what elevates this from merely funny to very funny.

jokes.text: Buddy: 'Is it okay if I finish?' / Amy: 'I'm not your mother.'
jokes.setup: Amy has spent their intimate moment in a policy monologue
jokes.punchline: Buddy asks permission to continue; Amy's 'I'm not your mother' is the most dismissive possible consent — granting permission through the disavowal of maternal authority
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy joke. Amy's 'I'm not your mother' functions simultaneously as: (a) consent, (b) a reminder that she won't be nurturing about it, (c) a characteristically cold Amy statement. The disconnect between what's happening and the clinical dismissiveness of her response is the joke. High quotability — this is a line people will remember.

jokes.text: Amy: 'There shouldn't even be a board.' Buddy: 'Is it okay if I finish?' Amy: 'I'm not your mother.'
jokes.setup: Amy has gone fully off-script into genuine policy anger during sex
jokes.punchline: Buddy's plaintive 'Is it okay if I finish?' is answered with the completely non-sequitur 'I'm not your mother' — Amy is so lost in her regulatory fury she can't even connect to the context
jokes.explanation: The capper to the dirty talk sequence. 'I'm not your mother' in response to 'Is it okay if I finish?' is both completely wrong as an answer and reveals Amy doesn't understand what he's asking because she's still in policy mode. Maximum economy — four words end the entire scene. Justifies 8.4 craft.

jokes.text: Ben visits Selina. They reconnect over Uber. Ben: 'A bunch of dumb-ass millennials, you know, too lazy to learn how to drive drunk.'
jokes.setup: Selina expresses sympathy about Ben losing his Uber job
jokes.punchline: Ben's analysis of why Uber failed him is that millennials are 'too lazy to learn how to drive drunk' — a completely inverted logic that frames drunk driving as a skill that should be cultivated
jokes.explanation: Sharp subversive logic. Ben frames drunk driving as a lost craft the next generation is failing to maintain. The 'dumb-ass millennials' framing is slightly clichéd but the 'drive drunk' twist is genuinely original.

jokes.text: Selina suggests Sherman Tanz as a donor: 'What if I told you that I had Tanz on board, hypothetically?' Ben: 'Shlomo Tanz is radioactive. He can read the newspaper on the toilet by the light of his own shit.'
jokes.setup: Selina floats Tanz as a potential donor/backer
jokes.punchline: Ben's insult implies Tanz is so corrupt he glows with his own moral waste — bioluminescent with shit
jokes.explanation: 'He can read the newspaper on the toilet by the light of his own shit' is a genuinely original insult. The image is precise, memorable, and funny. Ben's colorful condemnations are a consistent high point in Veep. Easily quotable.

jokes.text: Ben: 'I cannot. I'm... I'm out, okay? May God forgive me. I... I'm taking the Jonah job with Kent. I'm sorry, ma'am. I can't watch you lose again. There's nobody out there who wants to see a Meyer comeback, Selina. It's... it's over.'
jokes.setup: Selina has floated her presidential comeback to Ben
jokes.punchline: Ben's most devastating line is 'I can't watch you lose again' — the implicit acknowledgment that he has already watched her lose, combined with the utter hopelessness of 'nobody out there wants to see a Meyer comeback'
jokes.explanation: Darkly comic precisely because it's devastatingly true. 'May God forgive me' for taking the Jonah Ryan job signals how low the Jonah gig is. The mixture of cruelty and pity in Ben's assessment is well-written.

jokes.text: Selina immediately backtracks: 'Well, I was speaking hypothetically. I mean, you of all people should know that, Ben. Jesus Christ... I wanted you to come here because I was wondering if you wanted to join the board of my Meyer Fund for... AIDS and Adult Literacy.'
jokes.setup: Ben has rejected Selina's presidential comeback and said it's over
jokes.punchline: Selina instantly retreats to the AIDS and Adult Literacy charity as her actual reason for the meeting — a complete reversal delivered with wounded dignity
jokes.explanation: The AIDS charity as Selina's face-saving retreat is the running gag's fourth payoff. 'AIDS and Adult Literacy' intoned with dignity after being told her career is over is a solid comedic beat.

jokes.text: Ben: 'You know, maybe if we teach them how to read a condom wrapper, they wouldn't get AIDS in the first place.' Selina: 'Okay. See? There's hope.' Ben: 'Mm.'
jokes.setup: Ben has accepted the AIDS/Adult Literacy board position
jokes.punchline: Ben synthesizes both charities into a unified theory — literacy would prevent AIDS via condom wrapper reading — and Selina takes this dark joke as genuine hope
jokes.explanation: The perfect landing for the AIDS/Literacy running gag. Ben's synthesis — teach them to read a condom wrapper → no AIDS — is darkly logical, economical, and somehow hopeful, which is why Selina latches onto it. 'See? There's hope.' is the perfect coda. This is what a running gag payoff should look like — all the previous setup makes this land harder. Justifies 8.3 craft.

jokes.text: Ben: 'You know, ma'am, you're one of only 46 people who became...' Selina: 'Good night, Ben. Good night, good night. Good night.'
jokes.setup: Ben tries to offer a consoling historical perspective on the presidency
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately cuts him off and repeats 'good night' to end the conversation — she doesn't want the consolation speech; she wants the presidency
jokes.explanation: Selina can't bear to be told she should appreciate what she had — cutting Ben off before he can finish the 'you're one of only 46 presidents' speech is a nice character beat. The triple 'Good night' is a good button.

jokes.text: Richard: 'Ma'am, we're all set for private jets to Omaha on Monday and back from Council Bluffs on Wednesday.' Selina: 'We're coming back from Omaha on Monday.' Richard: 'Uh-oh.'
jokes.setup: Richard has booked the logistics for the Iowa trip, including the return from Council Bluffs
jokes.punchline: Selina orders him to book the return from Omaha (not Council Bluffs), maintaining the fiction that this is just a speech. Richard's 'Uh-oh' shows he understands exactly what this means
jokes.explanation: Richard's 'Uh-oh' is the perfect understated reaction — he knows exactly what's happening and processes it with gentle concern rather than alarm. This ties the episode's major arc together with one word.

jokes.text: End credits: 'CBS This Morning with Jane McCabe. And introducing Danny Egan.' Dan: 'I'm Jane McCabe.' Dan: 'And I'm Dan-ny Egan.' (said with odd emphasis on the second syllable)
jokes.setup: The show's title card and Dan's on-air introduction of himself
jokes.punchline: The credit card says 'introducing Danny Egan' (using the baby name 'Danny'); Dan introduces himself as 'Dan-NY' with odd emphasis — he hates 'Danny' but has accepted it
jokes.explanation: The 'Danny' vs 'Dan' distinction being encoded in the on-screen credit is a visual joke about Dan's vanity being unable to win even against network branding. Works better on rewatch.

jokes.text: On the commercial flight: flight attendant tells Selina's bag needs to be gate-checked. 'It's complimentary.' Flight attendant doesn't understand. Selina: 'I need hand sanitizer.'
jokes.setup: Selina (a former president) is on a commercial flight — already an indignity
jokes.punchline: The gate-check interaction and 'I need hand sanitizer' — the horror of being treated like a regular passenger — is the comedy. Her status has been reduced to commercial travel and she's visibly horrified
jokes.explanation: The final image of the episode — Selina Meyer on a commercial flight demanding hand sanitizer — is a perfect comedic summary of her current status. The gate-check interaction is the final indignity of an episode full of them. Works as a visual callback to all the private jet negotiation earlier.

tmdb_overview: One year after losing the presidency, Selina attempts to secure her legacy. Dan tries to ingratiate himself at his new job. Amy's brash D.C.-style personality proves too much for her new co-workers.
insight: Season 6 opener lands 53 jokes in 37 minutes—character chaos sustains momentum.
